participants.extracted: The study included 58 Mexican American families of children with clinically significant behavior problems treated at a community mental health clinic. Children were eligible if they were identified by a parent as Mexican American, were 3 to 7 years old, and scored above the clinical cutpoint on the Eyberg Child Behavior Inventory Intensity Scale. Most respondents were female primary caregivers, primarily biological mothers, with a few grandmothers, aunts, or other caregivers; male caregivers participated in some assessments and sessions but were too few for separate outcome reporting. The sample was predominantly male children, with a mean age of 52.8 months at baseline. Most families preferred Spanish, most caregivers were first-generation Americans, and many parents had a high school education or less. Families were randomized to GANA, standard PCIT, or treatment as usual, and the three groups did not differ significantly on demographic characteristics.

interventions.extracted: The study compared three interventions. Parent-Child Interaction Therapy (PCIT) taught parents skills to strengthen the parent-child relationship, increase prosocial behavior, and reduce disruptive behavior through Child-Directed Interaction and Parent-Directed Interaction phases, with therapists coaching parents toward mastery until treatment criteria were met. Guiando a Niños Activos (GANA) was a culturally adapted version of PCIT that retained the core PCIT components but added modifications to improve fit for Mexican American families, including framing the program as an educational/skill-building intervention, using culturally tailored videos and handouts, increasing therapist training in Mexican American culture, allowing extra time for rapport building, eliciting complaints each session, addressing beliefs about discipline and extended family, and implementing a comprehensive engagement protocol targeting mothers, fathers, and grandparents. Treatment as Usual (TAU) consisted of services provided by bilingual clinic therapists without PCIT training, using eclectic approaches such as person-centered cognitive behavioral, trauma-focused cognitive behavioral, and family systems therapy, with therapists having freedom to determine the number and type of sessions.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure child behavior and related family outcomes at pretreatment and long-term follow-up (collected on average 15.9 months post-treatment, range 6.58 to 24.47 months). Child behavior outcomes included the Eyberg Child Behavior Inventory (ECBI) Intensity Scale and Problem Scale, the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) Total Problems, Externalizing Problems, and Internalizing Problems scales, and the Early Childhood Inventory-4 (ECI-4) parent-report modules for ADHD, oppositional defiant disorder (ODD), and conduct disorder (CD). Family/parent outcomes included the Parenting Stress Index-Short Form (PSI-SF) total stress score and the Parental Locus of Control (PLOC) total score. These measures were assessed at baseline (Time 1) and again at long-term follow-up (Time 3) after treatment.
